Re:Zero — Learning JS in Another World, Day 1: What Even Is JavaScript?
Day one of getting reincarnated into the JS world. Just like dropping into an isekai, the first thing you've got to do on day one is understand the background of this world.
Before you start learning any language, it's usually a good idea to get a feel for where it came from. So on reincarnation day one, I'll give you a quick tour of JavaScript's history.

First off, the most common misunderstanding is that JavaScript has something to do with Java. Early on, JavaScript was actually called Mocha. Around that time there was another language getting really popular — Java — and to ride that wave, Mocha got renamed to JavaScript. The name before that was, if I remember right, LiveScript. There's a much more detailed write-up on this over on iThelp from kuro, so I won't go too deep here.
Getting to Know JavaScript Again: Day 02 — Intro to JavaScript (article in Mandarin)
So does JavaScript actually have anything to do with Java? Nope, nothing at all. They just slapped "Java" on the name to catch the hype of the moment.

Early JavaScript had a pretty simple job — it was just there to manipulate HTML. So back in the day JavaScript wasn't taken all that seriously, and most people building web pages leaned on backend languages like PHP or C# instead.

On top of that, early JavaScript didn't really have a strict spec. It wasn't until an international body called ECMAScript came along to define JavaScript that things got organized — before that, writing JavaScript was honestly kind of a free-for-all (wait, what?!). And ECMAScript has kept adding features to JavaScript ever since, like strict mode, constants, and a bunch of other syntax we'll get to later.

ECMAScript fixed up a lot of what JavaScript was missing and made it stronger. But a standard with no implementation is, well, just a standard. Someone has to actually implement it — and that someone is the browser. Here's where it gets tricky though: every browser supports things a little differently, so not every part of an ECMAScript spec works everywhere.
as an example. The IE family has issues where it doesn't support that syntax at all:
So if your user base still has people on old versions of IE,
just won't work for them. To deal with this, devs usually reach for JavaScript compilers/transpilers that turn unsupported syntax into supported syntax — Babel being the classic example.

So what's jQuery, then? Basically, jQuery simplified a ton of the syntax we use — things like manipulating DOM elements, handling events, and so on — and it also smoothed over a lot of those browser-support problems for us. That's a bit vague, so let me show you. Here's how selecting an HTML element looks with native syntax versus jQuery.

Day one of getting reincarnated into the JS world. Just like dropping into an isekai, the first thing you need to do is understand the lay of the land. So this opening post covers where JavaScript came from, why it has nothing to do with Java, what ECMAScript is, and how jQuery fits into the picture.
